feed.title: Combined summary - Post-Quantum HD-Wallets, Silent Payments, Key Aggregation, and Threshold Signatures

entry.title: Combined summary - Post-Quantum HD-Wallets, Silent Payments, Key Aggregation, and Threshold Signatures
entry.summary: The exploration and development of lattice-based cryptographic structures mark a significant advancement in enhancing security and privacy within blockchain technologies. These innovations are crucial for developing mechanisms that are resilient against quantum computing threats, a growing concern in the digital security domain. The research introduces various components such as Hierarchical Deterministic (HD) wallets, stealth addresses, key aggregation, and threshold signatures, which are foundational to the security and privacy enhancements in blockchain technology. A piece of notable research titled "[A Secure Hierarchical Deterministic Wallet with Stealth Address from Lattices](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0304397524002895?via%3Dihub)" highlights a deterministic tree structure based on basis-delegation and a static public identifier, facilitating the generation of one-time addresses by any sender to ensure transaction privacy. However, there's an acknowledgment of limitations concerning the practicality of signature and key sizes for blockchain systems, indicating a need for further efficiency improvements. Furthermore, the study "[Round-Optimal Secure Multisignature Schemes from Lattices with Public Key Aggregation and Signature Compression](https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-030-51938-4_14)" demonstrates the potential for consolidating multiple lattice public keys into a single key alongside a compressed signature. This suggests an advancement towards more efficient multi-signature schemes, although the exact parameters and practical implications regarding key and signature sizes remain vague without concrete values provided. In contrast, "[Efficient Multi-Signature Scheme Using Lattice](https://academic.oup.com/comjnl/article-abstract/65/9/2421/6289877?redirectedFrom=fulltext)" proposes a more refined approach towards multi-signature schemes, aiming to address efficiency concerns. Yet, the reliance on a trusted third party for generating public parameter sets raises questions about trust and potential risks associated with collusion or misbehavior. Lastly, "[Finally! A Compact Lattice-Based Threshold Signature](https://eprint.iacr.org/2025/872)" introduces a compact threshold signature scheme that maintains the signature size comparable to a single Dilithium signature for a certain number of signatories, despite an increase in the public key size. This development is particularly noteworthy for its clever approach to managing signature and public key sizes, even though the public keys are significantly larger than those in ML-DSA. These collective advancements underscore the potential compatibility of lattice-based cryptographic mechanisms with existing Bitcoin Improvement Proposals (BIPs) and other cryptographic protocols, suggesting no fundamental barriers to integrating post-quantum primitives with current standards. However, the critiques and observations regarding the practicality of key and signature sizes, along with the complexity and additional security requirements introduced by these schemes, highlight the ongoing challenges and areas for future work in making lattice-based cryptography viable for widespread adoption in securing blockchain technologies against quantum threats.
